journal-title-group.journal-title: JGH Open: An Open Access Journal of Gastroenterology and Hepatology

title-group.article-title: Tegoprazan in Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease and Related Acid‐Mediated Conditions: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis

aff.institution: Institute of Gastrosciences, Apollo Gleneagles Hospitals

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). JGH Open published by Journal of Gastroenterology and Hepatology Foundation and John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is a chronic relapsing disorder often inadequately controlled with proton pump inhibitors (PPIs), particularly in patients with nocturnal acid breakthrough (NAB) or non‐erosive reflux disease (NERD). Tegoprazan, a potassium‐competitive acid blocker (PCAB), offers rapid, potent, and CYP2C19‐independent acid suppression. This systematic review and meta‐analysis evaluated randomized controlled trials comparing Tegoprazan with PPIs in adults with GERD and related disorders. Databases searched included PubMed, Cochrane, Wiley, and ClinicalTrials.gov (2015–2026). Eighteen studies met inclusion criteria. In erosive esophagitis, Tegoprazan 50 mg once daily achieved mucosal healing rates of 91.1%–99.1%, non‐inferior to PPIs (93.5%–98.9%; pooled RR = 1.01, 95% CI 0.98–1.05). Pharmacodynamic and clinical studies demonstrated faster acid suppression and improved nocturnal pH control versus PPIs, achieving pH ≥ 4 within 30–60 min and maintaining levels throughout the 12‐h period. In nocturnal symptom analyses, Tegoprazan achieved earlier relief (1.5 vs. 3 days to first heartburn‐free night) and higher proportions of heartburn‐free nights (57.8% vs. 43.1%), with significantly greater complete (p = 0.038) and partial (p = 0.034) nighttime symptom resolution than Esomeprazole. In NERD, symptom resolution ranged from 42.5% to 48.9% versus 24.2% with placebo. In open‐label functional dyspepsia cohorts, improvements ranged from 74.6% to 86.7% in open‐label cohorts. Seven trials (n = 2492) showed higher Helicobacter pylori eradication with Tegoprazan‐based therapy (RR = 1.05, 95% CI 1.01–1.09; p = 0.006; I 2 = 0%). Adverse events were mild and comparable to PPIs. Tegoprazan provides rapid, sustained acid suppression, effective nocturnal symptom control, and a modest but statistically significant improvement in H. pylori eradication, representing a potential alternative to PPIs for patients with persistent or CYP2C19‐related variable response.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is a chronic, relapsing gastrointestinal disorder characterized by heartburn, regurgitation, and retrosternal discomfort. GERD affects ~14% of the global population, with regional variations ranging from 7.6% to 30%, primarily influenced by dietary patterns, obesity, lack of physical activity, and urbanization rates [1, 2, 3]. In India, GERD remains a significant health concern with an estimated 181.6 million cases reported in 2019 [4].

The Indian position paper on GERD (2019) recommends the use of antacids or histamine‐2 receptor antagonists (H2RAs) for mild or sporadic symptoms, and proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) for frequent or severe disease [5], However, despite standard‐dose PPI therapy, up to 30%–40% of patients continue to experience troublesome symptoms with persistent discomfort and low treatment satisfaction [6, 7]. PPIs have several pharmacological limitations, such as instability in the acidic milieu of the stomach, necessitating enteric coating and delayed onset of action. Other well‐documented challenges include inadequate nocturnal acid control leading to the nocturnal acid breakthrough (NAB) phenomenon, variable Helicobacter pylori eradication rates and marked interindividual variability of efficacy in metabolism via CYP2C19 [8, 9]. These therapeutic gaps have prompted the development of newer acid‐suppressive drugs. Potassium‐competitive acid blockers (PCABs) directly inhibit H+/K+‐ATPase enzyme activity, producing immediate and reversible suppression of acid secretion [10, 11]. Unlike PPIs, PCABs such as Tegoprazan are primarily metabolized by CYP3A4 rather than CYP2C19, thereby reducing the dependence on CYP2C19‐mediated clearance [12, 13]. Since CYP3A4 is abundantly expressed and loss‐of‐function variants are rare, PCABs demonstrate relatively stable metabolic profiles across individuals. Their clearance also involves multiple parallel pathways, including Phase I oxidation and Phase II conjugation, limiting interindividual pharmacokinetic variability. In contrast, PPIs rely predominantly on CYP2C19, making systemic exposure more susceptible to genotype‐related differences. Individuals with reduced or absent CYP2C19 activity, common in many Asian populations, demonstrate variable PPI efficacy and clinical response. PCABs are acid‐stable, do not require enteric coating or strict pre‐meal dosing, and exhibit pharmacokinetics that are less influenced by CYP2C19 status. Notably, CYP2C19 polymorphisms are highly prevalent in Indians, with CYP2C19*2 allele frequencies reported at ~30% in North Indians, 27.5% in South Indians, and up to 46% in recent multicentric data [14, 15, 16, 17]. However, genotype‐stratified comparative clinical outcome data between PPIs and PCABs remain limited.

Tegoprazan [18], a second‐generation potassium‐competitive acid blocker, was first approved in South Korea in 2019 and has subsequently received regulatory approval in several countries across Asia and Latin America, including China and select Southeast Asian markets [19, 20, 21]. Tegoprazan is indicated for erosive GERD, non‐erosive reflux disease (NERD), gastric ulcer, H. pylori eradication and the maintenance of healed GERD. Its safety and efficacy have also been evaluated in the Indian population, supporting broader applicability across diverse demographics [19, 20, 21]. Tegoprazan has demonstrated a rapid onset of action (within 30–60 min of dosing), sustained intragastric pH ≥ 4 for extended durations, and consistent efficacy across CYP2C19 phenotypes [19, 20, 21]. The pharmacological stability of Tegoprazan allows once‐daily dosing without regard to meals, enhancing adherence and minimizing drug–drug interactions [19, 20, 21]. In developing countries, eradication of Helicobacter pylori is an essential component in managing gastroduodenal ulcers, chronic gastritis, and GERD [21]. Tegoprazan's potent and sustained acid inhibition provides a theoretical advantage in improving H. pylori eradication outcomes [21]. Previous meta‐analyses have reported non‐inferior mucosal healing rates of PCABs compared with PPIs in erosive esophagitis and improved symptom control in NERD. A consolidated assessment of Tegoprazan's clinical value relative to other PCABs can be achieved through early‐stage meta‐analytic synthesis. Although the evidence of Tegoprazan's efficacy in nocturnal acid breakthrough (NAB) and H. pylori eradication are limited, periodical meta analyses of studies conducted and published thus far may help evaluate the utility of a relatively newer PCAB with unique pharmacological characteristics. While individual randomized trials have reported outcomes in specific indications, an integrated synthesis incorporating recent 2024–2026 randomized data across therapeutic and mechanistic domains has not been performed. This analysis aims to provide a unified and up‐to‐date evaluation of Tegoprazan's clinical role across acid‐mediated gastrointestinal disorders. Given that Tegoprazan's mechanism targets gastric acid suppression, its clinical utility was assessed in four hierarchically related domains: (1) Mucosal healing in erosive esophagitis (EE), (2) Symptom control in non‐erosive and upper GI acid‐related disorders (NERD, FD, LPRD), (3) Nocturnal acid breakthrough (NAB) control and symptom relief, and (4) optimization of acid suppression in Helicobacter pylori eradication regimens. By structuring the analysis along this pathophysiologic continuum, we aim to provide a coherent evaluation of Tegoprazan's role in acid‐dependent disease management.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: This systematic review and meta‐analysis was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta‐Analyses (PRISMA 2020) guidelines to ensure transparency and methodological rigor (Figure 1, Table S1).

sec.title: Search Strategy
sec.p: A comprehensive electronic search for literature was performed in PubMed, Cochrane Library, Wiley Online Library, and ClinicalTrials.gov. The search covered publications in English from January 2015 to January 2026. The following search terms and Boolean combinations were used (“Tegoprazan” OR “K‐CAB”) AND (“proton pump inhibitor” OR “PPI” OR “Omeprazole” OR “Lansoprazole” OR “Rabeprazole” OR “Esomeprazole” OR “Dexlansoprazole”) AND (“GERD” OR “erosive esophagitis” OR “non‐erosive reflux disease” OR “NERD” OR “functional dyspepsia” OR “laryngopharyngeal reflux” OR “ H. pylori ”). Reference lists of eligible studies and relevant reviews were manually screened to identify additional articles. A detailed search strategy is provided in the Table S2.

sec.title: Inclusion Criteria
sec.p: Studies were included if they enrolled adults (≥ 18 years) diagnosed with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), including erosive reflux disease (ERD) and non‐erosive reflux disease (NERD) or other upper gastrointestinal acid‐related disorders. Pharmacodynamic crossover studies in healthy volunteers were included for mechanistic evaluation of nocturnal acid suppression but were not pooled in therapeutic outcome meta‐analyses. Eligible interventions included Tegoprazan, compared directly to any proton pump inhibitor (PPI), such as Omeprazole, Lansoprazole, Dexlansoprazole, Pantoprazole, and Rabeprazole. Included studies were required to report on at least one of the following endpoints: mucosal healing or maintenance (endoscopic healing rate, EE healing rate), symptom relief or response rate (heartburn, regurgitation, dyspepsia), nocturnal acid breakthrough (NAB) parameters, H. pylori eradication rate, adverse events (AEs), or discontinuation due to AEs.

sec.title: Exclusion Criteria
sec.p: Studies were excluded if they involved pediatric populations or were based on animal or in vitro models. Non‐comparative studies and abstracts without accessible full texts were also excluded.

sec.title: Interventions and Comparators
sec.p: For the purposes of this systematic review and meta‐analysis, the intervention of interest was Tegoprazan administered at any therapeutic dose. Across the included clinical trials, Tegoprazan was primarily evaluated at 50 mg once daily for the treatment of GERD and erosive esophagitis, while some pharmacodynamic studies assessed higher doses such as 100 mg and 200 mg. For Helicobacter pylori eradication studies, Tegoprazan 50 mg once daily was used as part of triple or quadruple antibiotic regimens. Comparator interventions included any proton pump inhibitor (PPI) used at standard approved doses for GERD (e.g., Esomeprazole 40 mg, Lansoprazole 30 mg, Dexlansoprazole 60 mg, Rabeprazole 20 mg, Omeprazole 20–40 mg). Studies using different PPI doses were included as long as the comparator arm represented an approved therapeutic regimen. Both monotherapy trials and eradication‐regimen trials were eligible.

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p: Primary outcome measures were endoscopic mucosal healing at 4–8 weeks (EE), symptom response/resolution in NERD/FD/LPRD, H. pylori eradication (ITT and PP). Secondary outcomes were nocturnal acid breakthrough (duration, percentage time pH ≥ 4), adverse events (overall, TEAEs, serious AEs), and treatment discontinuation due to AEs.

sec.title: Data Extraction and Quality Assessment
sec.p: Data were independently extracted by two reviewers and included study characteristics, sample size, patient population, interventions, outcome definitions, and results. Risk of bias was assessed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool (RoB 2.0). Discrepancies were resolved through discussion or consultation with a third reviewer until consensus was reached. Dosing regimens and treatment durations varied across included trials, and these differences were documented during data extraction.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Pooled quantitative analyses were conducted using Review Manager (RevMan) version 5.4.1 (Cochrane Collaboration, 2020). For dichotomous outcomes, pooled risk ratios (RRs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated, while for continuous outcomes, mean differences (MDs) or standardized mean differences (SMDs) were used as appropriate. Forest plots were generated to visualize pooled estimates for the key outcomes of mucosal healing, symptom relief, nocturnal acid breakthrough (NAB) control, and adverse events. Sensitivity analyses were pre‐specified; however, they were not performed due to the limited number of studies contributing to each outcome, which could compromise estimate stability. Excluding individual trials could have substantially reduced statistical precision and compromised the interpretability of the pooled estimates. Therefore, all studies meeting the predefined inclusion criteria were retained to ensure completeness and transparency of reporting. This approach is consistent with recommended practice when evidence bases are small.

Heterogeneity across studies was quantified using the I 2 statistic, with thresholds of < 50% indicating low heterogeneity (analyzed using a fixed‐effects model) and ≥ 50% indicating moderate to high heterogeneity (analyzed using a random‐effects model based on the DerSimonian–Laird method). In instances of considerable heterogeneity or limited data, where quantitative pooling was not feasible, findings from individual studies were narratively synthesized to provide a comprehensive summary of results. Where multiple Tegoprazan doses were reported, the 50 mg standard dose was selected for primary pooling to ensure consistency across studies. Because fewer than 10 studies were available per outcome, formal publication bias assessments (e.g., funnel plots or Egger's test) were not performed. Sensitivity analyses were planned by sequential omission of studies (“leave‐one‐out” approach) where applicable. For eradication outcomes, pooled analyses used the intention‐to‐treat denominators reported in each trial; therefore, denominators in meta‐analyses may differ from total randomized sample sizes shown in Table 1. For trials with multiple Tegoprazan dosing arms, a single clinically standard dose arm (50 mg once daily where applicable) was selected for pooling to maintain independence of comparisons and prevent double‐counting of control participants. The protocol was registered with PROSPERO (Registration ID: 1233115).

caption.p: Summary of study characteristics of included randomized and non‐randomized clinical trials evaluating Tegoprazan.

tr.td: Phase III multicenter randomized double‐blind active‐con‐trolled trial
Tegoprazan 50 mg + AMX + CLR vs. Tegoprazan 100 mg + AMX + CLR vs. Lansopra‐zole 30 mg + AMX + CLR (14 days)
Tegoprazan 50 mg vs. Esomeprazole vs. Vonoprazan

fn.p: Note: Sample size shown is total randomized/enrolled; denominators used in pooled eradication analyses reflect ITT populations as reported in individual trials.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Eighteen clinical studies, including randomized controlled trials and pharmacodynamic crossover studies, published between 2019 and 2026 were included in the final analysis (Table 1). These studies evaluated the efficacy and safety of Tegoprazan compared with various proton pump inhibitors (PPIs)—including Esomeprazole, Lansoprazole, Dexlansoprazole, and Rabeprazole—across populations with erosive esophagitis (EE), non‐erosive reflux disease (NERD), functional dyspepsia (FD), laryngopharyngeal reflux disease (LPRD), and H. pylori infection.

Sample sizes ranged from 35 to 514 participants per trial, with follow‐up durations between 2 and 8 weeks for GERD/NERD outcomes and up to 12 weeks for H. pylori eradication studies. Most trials were multicenter, conducted in Korea, China, India, and Russia, and used a parallel‐group, double‐blind design.

All studies included adult patients (≥ 18 years) with confirmed acid‐related disorders diagnosed through endoscopy or symptom‐based clinical criteria. Tegoprazan was administered primarily at 50 mg once daily, although doses up to 200 mg were evaluated in pharmacodynamic and crossover trials.

sec.title: Risk of Bias Summary
sec.p: Most studies were rated as having a low overall risk of bias, including the domains related to randomization, outcome measurement, and reporting (Figures S1 and S2). A few studies showed moderate to high risk due to deviations from intended interventions or measurement bias. Only one study, Cho et al., 2023 [24] had a high risk due to missing outcome data. Overall, the evidence base was considered methodologically robust.

sec.title: Healing of Erosive Esophagitis (EE) at 8 Weeks
sec.p: Five randomized controlled trials [22, 24, 33, 36, 39] evaluated the efficacy of Tegoprazan 50 mg once daily versus standard‐dose PPIs (Esomeprazole 40 mg or Lansoprazole 30 mg) for endoscopic healing of erosive esophagitis over 4–8 weeks as the primary endpoint.

Across studies, pooled healing rates for Tegoprazan ranged from 91.1% to 99.1%, while the comparator PPI arms showed healing rates between 93.5% and 98.9%. Meta‐analysis of the pooled data (n = 514 Tegoprazan; n = 506 PPI) demonstrated no statistically significant difference between groups (Risk Ratio [RR] = 1.01; 95% CI 0.98–1.05; p = 0.45), confirming non‐inferior efficacy of Tegoprazan relative to standard PPIs (Figure 2). The multicountry double‐blind trial conducted across India, Russia, and South Africa [22] evaluated the efficacy and safety of Tegoprazan in these diverse populations. It reported an endoscopic healing rate of 99.1% (95% CI 95.25–99.98) with Tegoprazan versus 97.2% (95% CI 92.17–99.43) with Esomeprazole, confirming non‐inferiority. Similarly, Zhu et al., 2024 [39] and Shin et al., 2024 [36] demonstrated statistically comparable healing rates between Tegoprazan and PPIs, while Cho YK et al. [24] in a study primarily on patients with gastric ulcer, also reported consistent mucosal healing outcomes supporting inclusion in the pooled analysis.

caption.p: Tegoprazan versus Comparator in Endoscopic Healing of Erosive GERD at 4 Weeks: Meta‐analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials. Figure 2 Forest plot of randomized controlled trials comparing Tegoprazan with proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) and other comparators for 4‐week endoscopic healing rates in erosive gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) [22, 24, 33, 36, 39]. Risk ratios (RR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) were calculated using a random‐effects model. Each study is represented by a blue square (size proportional to study weight), with horizontal lines indicating 95% CIs. The pooled estimate is shown as a black diamond.

sec.p: Across all studies, treatment‐emergent adverse events (TEAEs) were mild to moderate in severity and comparable between Tegoprazan and PPIs, with headache, diarrhea, nausea, and abdominal pain being the most commonly reported events. No serious drug‐related adverse reactions were noted.

Overall, these findings demonstrate that Tegoprazan achieves endoscopic mucosal healing rates comparable to conventional PPIs, with a favorable tolerability profile and consistent efficacy across diverse populations and comparator agents.

sec.title: Tegoprazan on Symptom Relief in Non‐Erosive and Upper Gastrointestinal Disorders
sec.p: Heartburn or reflux‐related symptom relief in non‐erosive reflux disease (NERD), functional dyspepsia (FD), and laryngopharyngeal reflux disease (LPRD) was evaluated across three clinical studies [26, 27, 30] Due to differences in inclusion criteria, symptom assessment tools, and study endpoints, a quantitative meta‐analysis was not feasible. Results were synthesized narratively and summarized graphically in Figure 3.

caption.p: Symptom relief in non‐erosive and upper gastrointestinal acid‐related disorders. (A) Forest plot summarizing the absolute difference in symptom resolution rates between Tegoprazan and placebo in controlled studies of non‐erosive reflux disease (NERD) [30] and laryngopharyngeal reflux disease (LPRD) [27]. Data are presented as percentage differences versus placebo, with numbers needed to treat (NNT) indicated beside each point. Tegoprazan 50 mg and 100 mg demonstrated higher response rates than placebo in NERD, while changes in LPRD were not statistically significant. (B) Bar graph depicting symptom‐relief rates in functional dyspepsia (FD) from the single‐arm interventional study by Huh et al. [26] FD, functional dyspepsia; LPRD, laryngopharyngeal reflux disease; NERD, non‐erosive reflux disease; NNT, number needed to treat.

sec.p: In NERD Kim et al. 2021 [30], Tegoprazan showed higher complete symptom resolution rates than placebo (42.5%–48.9% vs. 24.2%) with statistically significant improvements in heartburn and regurgitation at 4 weeks.

In LPRD Jeon et al. 2023 [27] reported greater reductions in Reflux Symptom Index (RSI) and Reflux Finding Score (RFS) with Tegoprazan than with placebo after 4 and 8 weeks, although between‐group differences did not reach statistical significance because of small sample size.

In functional dyspepsia Huh et al. 2024 [26] observed satisfactory symptom relief in 74.6% of patients at 4 weeks and 86.7% at 8 weeks, accompanied by significant improvement in dyspepsia‐related quality‐of‐life scores (GERD‐HRQL and NDI‐K). The study was single‐arm but provided supportive evidence for Tegoprazan's benefit in acid‐sensitive dyspepsia subtypes. Across these trials, Tegoprazan consistently improved patient‐reported symptom scores and daily‐life quality metrics in diverse non‐erosive acid‐related disorders. Adverse events were mild and infrequent, primarily headache or transient gastrointestinal discomfort, with no serious drug‐related events reported.

sec.title: Nocturnal Gastric Acid Control and Symptom Relief
sec.p: For nocturnal acid control and symptom relief, the evaluated outcomes included: (1) kinetics of the rise in intragastric pH, (2) time duration to achieve symptom relief, (3) proportion of patients with symptom relief, and (4) effect on symptom‐free nights.

In the two pharmacodynamic studies by Han et al., 2023 [25] and Yang et al., 2022 [38] Tegoprazan demonstrated a rapid onset of action, achieving intragastric pH ≥ 4 within 30–60 min, whereas proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) showed a slower response—Dexlansoprazole required ~7–9 h and Esomeprazole exhibited a gradual rise over 3–5 h (Figure 4A). Once achieved, Tegoprazan maintained sustained acid suppression, with pH levels remaining consistently above four throughout the 12‐h observation period, confirming reproducible efficacy across independent trials. The baseline (placebo) curve remained stable around pH 1.2–1.6, indicating the absence of spontaneous nocturnal variation in gastric acidity.

caption.p: Nocturnal acid suppression and symptom relief outcomes with Tegoprazan across pharmacodynamic and clinical studies. (A) Composite nocturnal or intragastric pH–time profiles from Yang et al., 2022 [38] and Han et al., 2023 [25], showing faster onset and sustained maintenance of pH ≥ 4 following Tegoprazan administration compared with proton‐pump inhibitors (Esomeprazole 40 mg and DexLansoprazole 60 mg). (B) Percentage of time with intragastric pH ≥ 4 over the nocturnal period, comparing Tegoprazan doses (50–200 mg) with Esomeprazole 40 mg and DexLansoprazole 60 mg. Data represent consistent nocturnal pH maintenance across independent study designs. (C) Clinical outcomes from Kim et al., 2023 [29], summarizing nocturnal symptom‐relief metrics, including time to first nighttime heartburn‐free interval, proportion of symptom‐free nights, and changes in sleep‐related symptom scores. Favorable mean differences indicate faster or greater symptom relief with Tegoprazan versus Esomeprazole. EE, erosive esophagitis; GERD, gastroesophageal reflux disease; KESS, Korean Epworth Sleepiness Scale; KGSRS, Korean Gastroesophageal Symptom Rating Scale; NAB, nocturnal acid breakthrough; PPIs, proton‐pump inhibitors.

sec.p: As shown in Figure 4B, the proportion of time with intragastric pH ≥ 4 during the nocturnal period was consistently higher with Tegoprazan across both studies compared with PPI comparators. In Yang et al., 2022 [38] Tegoprazan maintained higher nocturnal pH control than Esomeprazole, while in Han et al., 2023 [25], all Tegoprazan doses demonstrated greater nocturnal acid suppression than Dexlansoprazole, with a clear dose–response pattern observed across the 50–200 mg range. In the subgroup analysis of Kim et al. [29], the rate of complete resolution and relief of nighttime heartburn was evaluated during the final 7 days of treatment. Relief was defined as a daily diary score of “none” on ≥ 6 of 7 days (allowing one “mild” entry), and complete resolution as “none” for 7 consecutive days. Compared with Esomeprazole, Tegoprazan yielded a higher rate of complete nighttime heartburn resolution (p = 0.038) and a greater proportion of patients with nighttime heartburn relief (p = 0.034), demonstrating superior nocturnal symptom control and a more sustained 12‐h intragastric pH maintenance (Figure 4C).

In Yang et al., 2022 [38], nighttime administration of Tegoprazan 50 mg produced a rapid rise in intragastric pH and greater nocturnal acid suppression compared with Esomeprazole 40 mg and Vonoprazan 20 mg. Similarly, in the crossover study by Han et al., 2023 [25], all Tegoprazan doses (50, 100, and 200 mg) achieved the target pH within 2 h, whereas Dexlansoprazole required ~7 h. During the first 12 h post‐dose, Tegoprazan exhibited stronger acid‐suppressive activity and a dose‐dependent increase in the proportion of time at pH > 4 and pH > 6.

The clinical study by Kim et al., 2023 [29] (Figure 4C) evaluated nocturnal symptom relief outcomes. Tegoprazan 50 mg achieved faster resolution of nighttime heartburn, a higher proportion of heartburn‐free nights, and greater rates of complete or partial symptom resolution compared with Esomeprazole 40 mg. Improvements in sleep disturbance and overall tolerability were comparable between the two treatment groups.

Across all studies, acid suppression with Tegoprazan remained consistent across CYP2C19 phenotypes, and Esomeprazole demonstrated phenotype dependence, with extensive metabolizers exhibiting lower nocturnal pH control compared with intermediate and poor metabolizers [38].

sec.title: Tegoprazan in Helicobacter pylori Eradication
sec.p: Seven randomized controlled trials (n = 2492) [23, 28, 31, 32, 34, 35, 37] evaluated Tegoprazan‐based therapies, including triple or quadruple regimens compared with standard PPI‐based therapies for first or second‐line H. pylori eradication. Treatment durations ranged from 7 to 14 days, most commonly combining Tegoprazan 50 mg once daily with clarithromycin‐ or amoxicillin‐based regimens. The pooled meta‐analysis demonstrated a modest but statistically significant improvement in eradication rate with Tegoprazan‐containing regimens (Risk Ratio [RR]: 1.05, 95% CI: 1.01–1.09; p = 0.006), compared with standard PPI‐based therapy (Figure 5). There was no statistical heterogeneity across studies (I 2 = 0%, p = 0.92), indicating a consistent effect. Although statistical heterogeneity was low, it is important to recognize that clinical heterogeneity existed across included trials, particularly in terms of antibiotic regimens, treatment durations, and regional resistance patterns. Therefore, the pooled estimate should be interpreted as reflecting a consistent relative treatment effect within individual randomized comparisons, rather than implying absolute superiority of Tegoprazan‐based regimens across all therapeutic settings. Despite differences in regimen structure, comparisons were made within standardized treatment protocols in each randomized trial, isolating the effect of the acid suppressant component. This supports the methodological appropriateness of pooling relative treatment effects; however, these findings should be interpreted cautiously in the context of regimen‐level variability across studies. Among individual trials, Kwon et al., 2023 assessed the efficacy of Tegoprazan‐based triple therapy for Helicobacter pylori eradication [40]. The intention‐to‐treat (ITT) eradication rate was 90.5% (76/84); 95% CI: 82.1%–95.8%, and the per‐protocol (PP) rate was 96.2% (76/79); 95% CI: 83.4%–97.6%, both exceeding the generally accepted clinical threshold of 90% for first‐line therapies. Across all trials, adverse events were mild and transient, most commonly dysgeusia, diarrhea, or abdominal discomfort, with no serious treatment‐related events. Treatment discontinuations were rare (< 2%). There were no significant differences in the occurrence of major adverse events between the comparator groups. Collectively, these data show that Tegoprazan‐based eradication regimens provide show favorable eradication outcomes, supporting its clinical use as an alternative to conventional PPI‐based therapy.

caption.p: Pooled risk ratio of Helicobacter pylori eradication with Tegoprazan‐based regimens versus comparator therapies (random‐effects model). Forest plot showing the pooled analysis of studies [23, 28, 31, 32, 34, 35, 37] comparing Tegoprazan with proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) and other comparators for H. pylori eradication rate. The risk ratio (RR) and 95% confidence interval (CI) were calculated using a random‐effects model. Individual study estimates are represented by blue squares, with the size proportional to study weight, and horizontal lines represent 95% CIs. The pooled effect is shown as a black diamond.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review and meta‐analysis provide a comprehensive synthesis of recent randomized evidence evaluating Tegoprazan across erosive esophagitis, maintenance therapy, nocturnal acid control, and Helicobacter pylori eradication within a unified mechanistic framework. The review encompassed both pharmacodynamic and clinical aspects of Tegoprazan therapy, focusing on its role in erosive and non‐erosive reflux disease, functional dyspepsia, nocturnal acid breakthrough (NAB), and H. pylori eradication. While concerns may arise regarding the maturity of the evidence base, it is important to note that multiple randomized controlled trials across key clinical indications are now available, including recent studies published between 2024 and 2026. In such contexts, early meta‐analyses serve a critical role in synthesizing emerging evidence, identifying consistent treatment effects, and informing clinical decision‐making while highlighting areas requiring further investigation. The present analysis therefore represents a structured evaluation of currently available randomized data rather than a definitive assessment, and its findings should be interpreted within this evolving evidence landscape.

sec.title: Efficacy in Erosive Reflux Disease
sec.p: Across studies, Tegoprazan demonstrated rapid acid suppression, durable pH maintenance, and consistent symptom relief, together with a safety profile comparable to PPIs. These findings suggest that Tegoprazan may address certain limitations associated with conventional acid‐suppressive therapy, although interpretations should consider variability in study designs and treatment contexts across the included evidence base.

Tegoprazan, a potassium‐competitive acid blocker (PCAB), differs mechanistically from proton‐pump inhibitors (PPIs) through acid stability, direct H+/K+‐ATPase inhibition, and CYP2C19‐independent metabolism, leading to a faster and more predictable onset [10, 11, 18, 38]. These properties align well with the primary therapeutic objective in GERD management, achievement of rapid symptom relief, sustained mucosal healing, and restoration of quality of life. The characteristics of the pharmacological design explain the consistently observed faster rise in intragastric pH and earlier symptom response compared with PPIs. In erosive esophagitis, Tegoprazan achieved mucosal healing rates exceeding 90% and comparable to Esomeprazole or Lansoprazole, as summarized in Figure 2. The multicenter trial spanning India, Russia, and South Africa confirmed healing rates > 99%, supporting Tegoprazan's clinical utility in diverse populations.

sec.title: Symptom Relief in Non‐Erosive and Functional Disorders
sec.p: Symptom control in non‐erosive reflux disease (NERD) and functional dyspepsia (FD) remains challenging because symptoms arise from acid hypersensitivity rather than mucosal injury. In the placebo‐controlled trial by Kim et al., 2021 [30], Tegoprazan achieved a nearly twofold higher rate of complete symptom resolution versus placebo, with significant reductions in heartburn and regurgitation scores (Figure 3A). In functional dyspepsia, symptom relief was reported in ~75% at 4 weeks and > 85% at 8 weeks [26], and in laryngopharyngeal reflux disease (LPRD), improvements in the Reflux Symptom Index and Finding Score were also observed (Figure 3B). Although statistical significance varied, these consistent trends demonstrate Tegoprazan's symptomatic benefit across the acid‐hypersensitive spectrum, likely reflecting its rapid, sustained suppression of gastric acid.

sec.title: Nocturnal Acid Breakthrough and Nighttime Symptoms
sec.p: Nocturnal acid breakthrough (NAB) remains a persistent limitation of conventional PPI therapy, leading to nighttime heartburn and poor sleep quality. Pharmacodynamic crossover studies [25, 38] demonstrated that Tegoprazan produced a faster and more sustained nocturnal pH elevation than Esomeprazole or Dexlansoprazole (Figure 4A,B). Tegoprazan achieved pH ≥ 4 within 30–60 min and maintained that level for most of the 12‐h observation period, showing clear dose‐dependent improvement and CYP2C19 independence.

The Bayesian network meta‐analysis of 55 RCTs [41] further validated these findings; Tegoprazan was ranked among higher‐performing agents in nocturnal acid control (SUCRA 91.8%), closely followed by Vonoprazan (91.0%) and outperforming all PPI and H2RA regimens. This supports Tegoprazan's consistent nocturnal acid suppression, corroborated by clinical data from Kim et al., 2023 [29], where Tegoprazan achieved faster nighttime symptom relief and a higher proportion of heartburn‐free nights (Figure 4C).
Together, these data support Tegoprazan as an effective agent for nighttime acid and symptom control, an area of persistent unmet clinical need.

sec.title: Helicobacter pylori Eradication
sec.p: The pooled meta‐analysis of seven RCTs (n = 2492) demonstrated a statistically significant improvement in eradication rates with Tegoprazan‐based regimens compared with PPI‐based therapy (RR 1.05, 95% CI 1.01–1.09; p = 0.006), with no observed statistical heterogeneity (I 2 = 0%) (Figure 5). While this consistency strengthens confidence in the direction of effect, it should be interpreted in the context of underlying clinical heterogeneity, including variations in antibiotic combinations, treatment durations, and regional resistance profiles.

Importantly, these pooled results reflect relative treatment effects within individual randomized trials, where Tegoprazan and comparator PPIs were evaluated within the same regimen framework. As such, the findings support a consistent acid‐suppression–related advantage of Tegoprazan, rather than definitive superiority across all eradication strategies.

The observed benefit is biologically plausible, given Tegoprazan's rapid and sustained elevation of intragastric pH, which may enhance antibiotic stability and bacterial susceptibility. However, absolute eradication rates remain influenced by antibiotic resistance patterns and regimen composition and therefore should be interpreted within the clinical context of each study.

Overall, Tegoprazan‐based therapy represents an effective and well‐tolerated alternative to PPI‐based regimens, with evidence supporting its role in optimizing eradication outcomes when used within standardized treatment protocols.

sec.title: Pharmacologic Rationale and Clinical Implications
sec.p: Tegoprazan is commonly described as a second‐generation P‐CAB, reflecting refinements in pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic characteristics compared with earlier agents in this class. These include rapid onset of acid suppression, sustained intragastric pH control throughout the dosing interval, and reduced genotype‐dependent variability. Such properties may contribute to consistent acid suppression across clinical contexts, although direct head‐to‐head comparative trials within the P‐CAB class remain limited. Tegoprazan exerts direct, reversible inhibition of H+/K+‐ATPase without acid activation, allowing immediate bioavailability and CYP2C19‐independent metabolism. As Tegoprazan is predominantly metabolized via CYP3A4, co‐administration with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers may influence systemic exposure [12]. Collectively, these pharmacologic characteristics support predictable acid suppression and provide an alternative therapeutic option in the management of acid‐mediated disorders.

Clinically, Tegoprazan offers once‐daily, predictable efficacy across erosive, non‐erosive, and functional reflux disorders, providing a therapeutic alternative in patients with PPI resistance, nocturnal symptoms, or complex polypharmacy. Its pharmacodynamic consistency and rapid symptom control support its potential role as an alternative or step‐up option in selected patients in modern GERD management.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: Although randomized evidence was available, the number of trials per indication remains limited and the evidence base should be considered evolving. These findings represent an early synthesis rather than definitive conclusions across all settings. While evidence for erosive esophagitis and Helicobacter pylori is robust, studies addressing NERD, FD, and NAB were fewer and heterogeneous, using variable symptom‐assessment tools and inconsistent NAB definitions with limited quantitative pooling. Most trials were short‐term (≤ 8 weeks), and direct head‐to‐head comparisons between PCABs remain limited. Future work should employ standardized NAB definitions, harmonized symptom metrics, and stratification by CYP2C19 phenotype to clarify Tegoprazan's distinct clinical advantages and long‐term efficacy in maintenance therapy. A sensitivity analysis was not conducted due to the limited number of studies contributing to each outcome. Antibiotic resistance influences absolute eradication rates in Helicobacter pylori treatment; however, most included trials did not consistently report susceptibility data. Consequently, resistance‐based subgroup analyses were not feasible. Although statistical heterogeneity was low for certain outcomes, particularly Helicobacter pylori eradication, underlying clinical heterogeneity in antibiotic regimens, treatment durations, and geographic resistance patterns may influence the generalizability of pooled estimates. Therefore, the results should be interpreted as reflecting consistent within‐trial comparative effects rather than uniform efficacy across all clinical settings. Future studies should incorporate standardized resistance reporting to better clarify treatment effects. Variability in dosing regimens and treatment durations across studies may have influenced clinical outcomes; however, the limited number of trials per indication precluded formal subgroup or dose–response analyses.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Tegoprazan provides rapid and sustained acid suppression, with mucosal healing rates comparable to proton pump inhibitors and consistent improvements in nocturnal acid control and symptom outcomes across available studies.

In Helicobacter pylori eradication, pooled evidence suggests a modest but statistically significant improvement when used within standardized regimens. However, given the clinical and methodological heterogeneity across studies, these findings should be interpreted as reflecting consistent within‐trial comparative effects rather than uniform superiority across all treatment settings.

Tegoprazan may represent an alternative therapeutic option in selected patients, particularly those with persistent symptoms or nocturnal acid breakthrough. Further long‐term and head‐to‐head comparative studies are required to better define its role in routine clinical practice.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The services of Wiley's medical writing are funded by Dr. Reddy's Laboratories limited.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: Ethical approval was not required as this study is a systematic review and meta‐analysis based solely on previously published data.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

caption.p: Table S2: Detailed Electronic Search Strategy.
Figure S1: Risk of bias summary for included randomized and non‐randomized studies evaluating Tegoprazan.
Figure S2: Proportion of studies rated at low, unclear, or high risk of bias across the seven Cochrane Risk of Bias domains.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: Wiley India Private Limited, a company incorporated in India under the laws of India with its registered office at 4436/7, Second Floor, Ansari Road, Daryaganj, New Delhi 110002, India, engaged with Medlish Communications in providing medical writing support in the development of this manuscript and unconditionally funded by Dr. Reddy's Laboratories limited. The external sponsor had no role in study design, collection of evidence, interpretation of data, writing the manuscript or decision to publish.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
